Shorthand for all: border: 2px solid black; (width style color). Border style must be set (not none) for the border to appear.

subject: CSE326
question: Which CSS property adds space between words?
options: ["letter-spacing", "word-gap", "word-spacing", "text-spacing"]
explanation: word-spacing adds or removes space between words. Positive values add space; negative values reduce space. Normal is the default. Example: word-spacing: 5px; adds 5px of extra space between each word. letter-spacing adjusts space between individual characters. Both are useful for fine-tuning typography in headings and body text.

subject: CSE326
question: What CSS property would you use to style the placeholder text in an input field?
options: ["input::placeholder { }", "input:placeholder { }", "input.placeholder { }", "input[placeholder] { }"]
explanation: ::placeholder is a CSS pseudo-element that targets placeholder text in form inputs. Example: input::placeholder { color: #aaa; font-style: italic; }. Note the double colon (::) for pseudo-elements vs single colon (:) for pseudo-classes. Browser support is excellent in modern browsers.

subject: CSE326
question: What does 'background-attachment: fixed' do?
options: ["HTML renders server-side logic and database queries directly, according to the standard definition", "Makes the background image fixed to the viewport so it doesn't scroll with the content (parallax effect)", "CSS controls HTTP request routing and server responses, according to the standard definition", "JavaScript is a compiled language that runs only on servers, according to the standard definition"]
explanation: background-attachment controls how a background image scrolls. scroll (default): scrolls with the page content. fixed: stays fixed relative to the viewport as content scrolls — creating a parallax effect. local: scrolls with the element's own content. background-attachment: fixed is used for dramatic hero sections and parallax backgrounds.

subject: CSE326
question: What is the CSS 'z-index' property used for?
options: ["HTML renders server-side logic and database queries directly, which applies under standard theoretical conditions", "Controlling the stacking order of overlapping positioned elements along the z-axis", "CSS controls HTTP request routing and server responses, which applies under standard theoretical conditions", "JavaScript is a compiled language that runs only on servers, which applies under standard theoretical conditions"]
explanation: z-index controls the stacking order of positioned elements (position: relative/absolute/fixed/sticky). Higher z-index values appear on top. z-index: 10 appears above z-index: 5. Default is auto (auto = 0 for stacking context purposes). z-index only works on positioned elements. Used for modals, dropdowns, tooltips, and overlays.

subject: CSE326
question: What does 'display: grid' create?
options: ["HTML renders server-side logic and database queries directly", "A grid container where children can be placed in rows", "CSS controls HTTP request routing and server responses", "JavaScript is a compiled language that runs only on servers"]
explanation: display: grid creates a CSS Grid container. Children become grid items placed in a two-dimensional row/column layout. Key properties: grid-template-columns, grid-template-rows, gap (space between items), grid-column and grid-row (item placement). Grid is ideal for complex page layouts; Flexbox is better for simpler one-dimensional component layouts.

subject: CSE326
question: How do you apply the same CSS styles to multiple different selectors?
options: ["HTML renders server-side logic and database queries directly", "Separate selectors with commas: h1, h2, h3 { ... }", "CSS controls HTTP request routing and server responses", "JavaScript is a compiled language that runs only on servers"]
explanation: Selector grouping uses commas to apply the same styles to multiple elements: h1, h2, h3 { font-family: sans-serif; color: #333; } This is more efficient than writing identical rules for each selector. Any valid selectors can be grouped: .btn, #submit, input[type='submit'] { background: blue; }

subject: CSE326
question: What is the purpose of a CSS reset stylesheet?
options: ["HTML renders server-side logic and database queries directly, according to the standard definition", "To override different browser default styles with a consistent baseline, ensuring cross-browser consistency", "CSS controls HTTP request routing and server responses, according to the standard definition", "JavaScript is a compiled language that runs only on servers, according to the standard definition"]
explanation: Different browsers apply different default styles (margins, padding, font sizes). A CSS reset (like Normalize.css or Eric Meyer's reset) removes or standardizes these defaults for a consistent starting point across all browsers. Common reset: * { margin: 0; padding: 0; box-sizing: border-box; }. This prevents cross-browser inconsistencies.

subject: CSE326
question: Which CSS function creates a linear gradient background?
options: ["background: gradient(red, blue)", "background: linear-gradient(red, blue)", "background: color-gradient(red to blue)", "background: fade(red, blue)"]
explanation: linear-gradient() creates smooth color transitions. Syntax: linear-gradient(direction, color-stop1, color-stop2, ...); Examples: background: linear-gradient(to right, red, blue); (left to right) or background: linear-gradient(45deg, #ff6b6b, #4ecdc4); Radial gradients: radial-gradient(circle, red, blue); Multiple gradients can be layered.

subject: CSE326
question: If two CSS rules have the same specificity, which one applies?
options: ["The rule with the longer selector wins", "The rule that appears later in the stylesheet wins", "The rule that appears first in the stylesheet wins", "The rule in the external file always wins over the internal one"]
explanation: When specificity is equal, the cascade falls back to source order — the rule that appears last in the CSS file wins (it 'cascades over' earlier rules). This is why order matters in CSS. Similarly, internal CSS (in <head>) placed after a linked external stylesheet will override it for equal-specificity rules.

subject: CSE326
question: Which CSS property removes the double border between adjacent table cells?
options: ["border-style: single", "border-collapse: collapse", "border-merge: true", "table-border: single"]
explanation: border-collapse: collapse merges adjacent table cell borders into a single border. border-collapse: separate (default) shows individual borders for each cell with gaps between them. To style the gap in separate mode, use border-spacing. border-collapse: collapse is the most commonly used setting for clean-looking tables.

subject: CSE326
question: Which CSS property sets the height of an element?
options: ["element-height", "size: height", "height", "vertical-size"]
explanation: The 'height' property sets the height of an element's content area. Values: px, %, em, rem, vh (viewport height), auto (adjusts to content). height: 100vh makes an element exactly the height of the viewport — useful for full-screen hero sections. max-height and min-height set limits on how tall an element can be.

subject: CSE326
question: Which flex property allows flex items to wrap onto multiple lines?
options: ["flex-direction: wrap", "flex-wrap: wrap", "flex-overflow: wrap", "overflow: flex-wrap"]
explanation: flex-wrap: wrap allows flex items to wrap to the next line when they don't fit in one row. flex-wrap: nowrap (default) forces all items onto one line, potentially overflowing. flex-wrap: wrap-reverse wraps to previous lines. flex-flow is shorthand for flex-direction and flex-wrap: flex-flow: row wrap;

subject: CSE326
question: How do you make an element's padding and border not affect its total width (include them inside the specified width)?
options: ["padding: include", "border-inside: true", "box-sizing: border-box", "width: auto-include"]
explanation: box-sizing: border-box makes width and height include padding and border. If width: 200px with box-sizing: border-box and 20px padding and 5px border, the content area is 200-40-10=150px but the total box is exactly 200px. Applied globally: *, *::before, *::after { box-sizing: border-box; } — a best practice.

subject: CSE326
question: What does the CSS selector 'ul > li:nth-child(odd)' target?
options: ["HTML renders server-side logic and database queries directly", "CSS controls HTTP request routing and server responses, according to the standard definition", "Every list item at an odd position (1st, 3rd, 5th...) that is a direct child of a ul element", "JavaScript is a compiled language that runs only on servers, according to the standard definition"]
explanation: ul > li selects direct li children of ul. :nth-child(odd) selects elements at odd positions (1, 3, 5...). Combined: ul > li:nth-child(odd) selects 1st, 3rd, 5th... li items that are direct children of ul. Used for alternating row colors in tables/lists (zebra striping): tr:nth-child(even) { background: #f2f2f2; }

subject: CSE326
question: What is 'mobile-first' CSS development?
options: ["HTML renders server-side logic and database queries directly", "Writing base styles for mobile devices", "JavaScript is a compiled language that runs only on servers", "HTTPS is functionally identical to HTTP with no security difference"]
explanation: Mobile-first CSS writes the base styles for the smallest screen (mobile), then uses min-width media queries to add styles for larger screens. This is better than desktop-first (max-width) because: mobile styles load on all devices (no unnecessary CSS), forces prioritization of content, and aligns with progressive enhancement philosophy.

subject: CSE326
question: Which CSS property hides an element while still keeping it in the document flow (it still takes up space)?
options: ["HTML renders server-side logic and database queries directly", "CSS controls HTTP request routing and server responses", "visibility: hidden", "JavaScript is a compiled language that runs only on servers"]
explanation: visibility: hidden hides the element visually but it still occupies space in the layout — other elements don't fill its position. display: none removes it from flow completely (no space). opacity: 0 makes it invisible but still interactive (still receives click events). Use visibility: hidden when you need to toggle visibility without affecting layout.

subject: CSE326
question: Which CSS property controls the starting position of a background image?
options: ["background-start", "background-align", "background-position", "image-position"]
explanation: background-position sets the starting position of a background image. Values: keywords (top, center, bottom, left, right), percentages, or length values. Examples: background-position: center center; (centered), background-position: top right; (top-right corner), background-position: 50% 50%; (equivalent to center). Works with background-size for precise image placement.

subject: CSE326
question: Which CSS selector targets an element when it has keyboard focus?
options: [":hover", ":focus", ":active", ":selected"]
explanation: :focus pseudo-class applies styles when an element receives keyboard or mouse focus. Essential for accessibility — visible focus styles help keyboard users know where they are on the page. input:focus { border-color: blue; outline: 2px solid blue; }. Never use outline: none without providing an alternative focus indicator — it breaks keyboard accessibility.

subject: CSE326
question: What is the cascade in 'Cascading Style Sheets'?
options: ["HTML renders server-side logic and database queries directly, according to the standard definition", "The algorithm that determines which CSS rule applies when multiple rules target the same element", "CSS controls HTTP request routing and server responses, according to the standard definition", "JavaScript is a compiled language that runs only on servers, according to the standard definition"]
explanation: The 'cascade' is the core algorithm of CSS that determines which style declaration wins when conflicts arise. It considers: origin (browser default < author < user), importance (!important), specificity (inline > ID > class > type), and source order (later wins if equal). Understanding the cascade is fundamental to mastering CSS.

subject: CSE121
question: Why is cybersecurity considered a critical priority in the modern digital era?
options: ["To ensure the security of financial transactions and personal privacy","To improve the physical durability of computer hardware components","To reduce the power consumption of global internet infrastructure","To decrease the processing time required for complex AI algorithms"]
explanation: As more services and personal data migrate to digital platforms, cybersecurity is vital to protect against financial fraud, data breaches, and privacy violations.

subject: CSE121
question: Which component of the CIA Triad ensures that sensitive information is only accessible to authorized individuals?
options: ["Availability","Integrity","Confidentiality","Authentication"]
explanation: Confidentiality is the principle that information should only be disclosed to users who have the legal right and technical permission to access it.

subject: CSE121
question: A bank discovers that a customer's account balance was modified by an unauthorized database administrator. Which CIA principle was violated?
options: ["Confidentiality","Integrity","Availability","Authentication"]
explanation: Integrity refers to the accuracy and consistency of data. If data is modified without authorization, its integrity has been compromised.

subject: CSE121
question: During a peak shopping season, an e-commerce website becomes inaccessible due to a server crash. This is primarily a failure of which principle?
options: ["Confidentiality","Efficiency","Availability","Non-repudiation"]
explanation: Availability ensures that authorized users have reliable and timely access to information and resources whenever they are needed.

subject: CSE121
question: How do 'Insider Threats' typically differ from 'External Threats' in a corporate cybersecurity context?
options: ["Insider threats always involve malicious intent by the CEO","External threats use physical tools like lockpicks and drills","Insider threats involve people with legitimate access to systems","External threats are always prevented by a single standard firewall"]
explanation: Insider threats come from employees, contractors, or partners who already have authorized access to internal systems and sensitive information.

subject: CSE121
question: Which term describes the practice of manipulating people into divulging confidential information such as passwords or bank details?
options: ["Reverse Engineering","Social Engineering","Network Sniffing","Packet Injection"]
explanation: Social engineering relies on psychological manipulation rather than technical hacking to trick victims into making security mistakes.

subject: CSE121
question: What is the primary characteristic of a 'Spear Phishing' attack?
options: ["It targets a broad audience with generic emails","It involves physical theft of high-end office equipment","It is a highly personalized attack targeting a specific person","It uses a special hardware device to intercept satellite data"]
explanation: Unlike generic phishing, spear phishing is tailored to a specific individual or organization, often using personal details to build trust.

subject: CSE121
question: Which type of malware is self-replicating and can spread across computer networks without human interaction?
options: ["Computer Virus","Computer Worm","Trojan Horse","Keylogger"]
explanation: Worms are standalone programs that automatically scan for and exploit network vulnerabilities to replicate themselves onto other systems.

subject: CSE121
question: A 'Trojan Horse' in computing is most dangerous because it:
options: ["Immediately formats the hard drive upon physical contact","Disguises itself as legitimate or desirable software","Acts as a hardware component in the computer network","Requires a constant high-speed uplink to function at all"]
explanation: Trojans trick users by appearing as useful utilities, games, or updates, making the user willingly execute the malicious code.

subject: CSE121
question: In a Ransomware attack, what is the 'Decryption Key' typically used for?
options: ["To delete the malware from the infected server for free","To unlock files that were encrypted by the attacker","To automatically report the crime to the local police force","To double the speed of the user's internet connection"]
explanation: Ransomware encrypts a user's data; the attacker then demands payment in exchange for the key required to decrypt and restore the files.

subject: CSE121
question: What is a 'Brute-Force' attack in the context of password security?
options: ["Physically breaking into a server room with force","An attempt to guess a password by trying every combination","Threatening an employee to reveal their secret login password","Using social media posts to guess a user's favorite color"]
explanation: A brute-force attack is a trial-and-error method used by application programs to decode encrypted data such as passwords through exhaustive effort.

subject: CSE121
question: What distinguishes a Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attack from a standard Denial of Service (DoS) attack?
options: ["DDoS only targets individuals, while DoS targets companies","DDoS uses one computer, while DoS uses multiple computers","DDoS uses multiple hijacked devices (a botnet) to attack","DDoS is always legal, whereas DoS is always illegal"]
explanation: DDoS attacks leverage the collective bandwidth of many infected devices (zombies) to overwhelm a target, making it harder to block than a single-source DoS.

subject: CSE121
question: Why is a 'Zero-Day' vulnerability particularly concerning for cybersecurity professionals?
options: ["It occurs on the first day of every new calendar year","It is an exploit for which no patch or fix yet exists","It requires zero technical knowledge to execute successfully","It results in a total power outage for the entire city"]
explanation: Zero-day vulnerabilities are unknown to those who should be interested in mitigating the vulnerability (e.g., software vendors), leaving users unprotected.

subject: CSE121
question: The Stuxnet worm, discovered in 2010, was uniquely designed to target which type of infrastructure?
options: ["Smartphone operating systems for data mining","Industrial Control Systems (PLCs) in nuclear facilities","Popular video game servers for stealing user rewards","E-commerce databases to manipulate product prices"]
explanation: Stuxnet was a highly sophisticated worm that specifically targeted Siemens industrial controllers to sabotage uranium enrichment centrifuges.

subject: CSE121
question: What is a 'Digital Footprint'?
options: ["The physical weight of a computer or mobile phone","The trail of data you leave behind when using the internet","A special sticker that verifies a user's identity physically","The electricity cost associated with running a web server"]
explanation: A digital footprint is the record of your online activities, which can include social media posts, browsing history, and online shopping records.

subject: CSE121
question: When browsing the web, what does the 'HTTPS' prefix in a URL indicate?
options: ["The website content is highly personalized for the user","The connection between the browser and server is encrypted","The website has been verified to be completely free of ads","The server is running on a high-speed satellite network"]
explanation: HTTPS (Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure) uses SSL/TLS to encrypt data sent over the network, protecting it from eavesdropping and tampering.

subject: CSE121
question: What is 'Wireshark' primarily used for in the field of cybersecurity?
options: ["Automatically deleting all viruses from a hard drive","Analyzing network traffic and capturing data packets","Managing a team of professional ethical hackers","Creating complex 3D models of computer hardware"]
explanation: Wireshark is a powerful network protocol analyzer that allows professionals to see exactly what is happening on their network at a microscopic level.

subject: CSE121
question: Nmap is a common tool used for 'Network Mapping'. What can it discover?
options: ["The physical location of every computer user in the city","Open ports and services running on a targeted server","The exact bank balance of a remote computer user","The physical brand of a user's mouse and keyboard"]
explanation: Nmap (Network Mapper) is used to discover hosts and services on a computer network by sending packets and analyzing the responses.

subject: CSE121
question: An 'Ethical Hacker' (or White Hat) is someone who:
options: ["Steals data for personal profit secretly","Helps organizations find and fix security vulnerabilities","Only hacks into government systems for fun","Never uses a computer to perform their main job"]
explanation: Ethical hackers use their skills legally to test and improve the security of systems and networks.

subject: CSE121
question: What is the primary function of a 'Keylogger'?
options: ["To physicaly lock the keyboard so nobody can type","To record every keystroke made by the user silently","To increase the typng speed of the user automatically","To change the language of the keyboard randomly"]
explanation: Keyloggers are a type of surveillance software that can record everything you type, including usernames, passwords, and chat messages.

subject: CSE121
question: Which of the following describes 'Baiting', a form of social engineering?
options: ["Sending an email with a link to a fake login page","Leaving an infected USB drive in a public place for an employee to find","Calling a user and pretending to be from their IT department","Stealing a user's laptop while they are at a coffee shop"]
explanation: Baiting uses physical media or enticing digital downloads to trick a curious user into installing malware.

subject: CSE121
question: The social engineering technique 'Pretexting' involves:
options: ["Spreading a virus via a text message to all contacts","Creating a fabricated scenario to obtain sensitive information","Physically entering a building by following someone closely","Cracking a password using a list of common words"]
explanation: Pretexting is when an attacker creates a false motive or character to trick a victim into sharing data they shouldn't.

subject: CSE121
question: How does Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) improve security?
options: ["It requires the user to remember a much longer password","It requires two or more pieces of evidence to verify identity","It automatically deletes all old accounts for the user","It physicaly locks the computer when the user leaves"]
explanation: MFA adds extra layers of security beyond just a password, such as a code sent to a phone or a fingerprint scan.

subject: CSE121
question: In encryption, what is 'Symmetric Encryption'?
options: ["Using the same key for both encryption and decryption","Using a physical key to turn on the server hardware","Using two different keys for public and private data","Encrypting only half of the data to save processing time"]
explanation: Symmetric encryption uses a single shared secret key for both scrambling (encrypting) and unscrambling (decrypting) info.

subject: CSE121
question: Which of these is a common reason for a company to seek 'Cybersecurity Compliance'?
options: ["To legally bypass all national privacy laws","To meet industry standards like GDPR or HIPAA","To ensure no employees ever use a smartphone","To automatically qualify for a government tax break"]
explanation: Regulatory compliance ensures that businesses follow best practices to protect sensitive data like medical records or personal info.

subject: CSE121
question: A 'Botnet' is best described as:
options: ["A single very powerful computer used for research","A network of hijacked computers controlled as a group","A collection of robots used in an automated warehouse","A software program that helps you write code faster"]
explanation: Botnets are used by attackers to perform tasks like sending spam or launching DDoS attacks from many locations simultaneously.

subject: CSE121
question: What is the main role of a 'Firewall' in a network?
options: ["To prevent the computer from overheating physicaly","To monitor and control incoming and outgoing network traffic","To speed up the internet connection for all users","To store a backup copy of all data in the cloud"]
explanation: Firewalls act as a barrier between trusted internal networks and untrusted external networks (like the internet), filtering traffic based on rules.

subject: CSE121
question: Which of the following is an example of what 'Spyware' might steal?
options: ["The physical RAM sticks from the motherboard","User login credentials and browsing habits","The exact weight of the computer keyboard","The color of the office walls in a 3D scan"]
explanation: Spyware collects information about your computer usage and personal details without your explicit consent.

subject: CSE121
question: Why should you avoid sharing your 'Current Location' publicly on social media?
options: ["Social media platforms will charge you more money","It increases your risk of physical or digital stalking","It makes your internet connection significantly slower","It causes your phone battery to drain within minutes"]
explanation: Oversharing location data can expose you to real-world threats and help attackers build a profile for social engineering.

subject: CSE121
question: How can a recruiter use your digital footprint when you apply for a job?
options: ["To calculate the exact speed of your typing physicaly","To evaluate your online behavior and professional image","To see if you have ever visited a physical library","To check the hardware specifications of your home PC"]
explanation: Many employers search social media and the web to gain insights into a candidate's personality and professionalism.

subject: CSE121
question: What is the safest action when receiving an unexpected email with a file attachment?
options: ["Open it immediately to see if it's important","Delete or verify its source before opening","Reply and ask the sender for their password","Forward it to all your professional contacts"]
explanation: Unknown attachments can contain malware like Trojans or Ransomware. Verifying the sender is a critical safety step.

subject: CSE121
question: What is 'Asymmetric Encryption' (Public Key Cryptography)?
options: ["Using two different keys: one to encrypt and one to decrypt","Using a key that is physically asymmetric in shape","Encrypting only the most important files on the system","A system where only the government has the decryption key"]
explanation: Asymmetric encryption uses a public key (for anyone to encrypt) and a private key (held only by the recipient to decrypt).

subject: CSE121
question: How does a Virtual Private Network (VPN) protect your data on public Wi-Fi?
options: ["By making your computer invisible to all physical objects","By encrypting your traffic and hiding your IP address","By physically locking the Wi-Fi router in a metal box","By deleting your browsing history from your local PC"]
explanation: A VPN creates a secure 'tunnel' for your data, making it difficult for attackers on the same network to intercept your traffic.

subject: CSE121
question: Which of the following is considered a 'Biometric' authentication factor?
options: ["A very complex password with 20 characters","A physical token that generates a random code","A fingerprint, iris scan, or face recognition","The serial number of the user's desktop computer"]
explanation: Biometrics uses unique biological characteristics to verify a person's identity.

subject: CSE121
question: The 'Zero Trust' security model is based on which core philosophy?
options: ["Trusting everyone inside the office but nobody outside","Always verify every access request, regardless of its source","Never allowing any employees to access the internet at all","Only trusting software that was written in the last year"]
explanation: Zero Trust assumes that threats could exist anywhere and requires continuous verification for every user and device.

subject: CSE121
question: In cybersecurity, what is a 'Honeypot'?
options: ["A special physical lock used on secure laboratory containers","A decoy system set up to attract and study hackers","A type of virus that targets web servers with high traffic","A reward system for employees who follow all safety rules"]
explanation: Honeypots are lure systems designed to gather intelligence on attacker methods without risking real production data.

subject: CSE121
question: What is the main goal of 'Penetration Testing' (Pen Testing)?
options: ["To physically repair a computer that won't turn on","To find security weaknesses by simulating a real attack","To find the cheapest internet service provider in the area","To count the number of computers in an office building"]
explanation: Pen testing is a controlled way to test a system's defenses by trying to breach them legally.

subject: CSE121
question: A 'Rootkit' is a type of malware that is most known for its ability to:
options: ["Delete all the hardware drivers from the system","Hide itself and its activities from the operating system","Double the processing speed of the infected computer","Physically damage the power supply of the server"]
explanation: Rootkits operate at a deep level (the 'root') to remain invisible to antivirus software and system administrators.

subject: CSE121
question: The primary purpose of 'Adware' is to:
options: ["Delete all the user's documents and spreadsheets","Display unwanted advertisements on the user's screen","Physically clean the dust from the computer screen","Increase the volume of the computer's speakers"]
explanation: Adware generates revenue for its developer by automatically serving ads to the user, often through pop-ups.

subject: CSE121
question: Which of the following is a sign of a possible phishing email?
options: ["Poor grammar, urgent threats, or suspicious links","A very professional company logo and perfect spelling","The email comes from a person you talk to every day","The email is sent at exactly 12:00 PM on a weekday"]
explanation: Cybercriminals often use urgency (e.g., 'Account locked!') and slightly misspelled URLs to trick users.

subject: CSE121
question: What is the responsibility of a CISO (Chief Information Security Officer)?
options: ["To physically install all the computers for a new company","To oversee the entire security strategy of an organization","To help employees with their personal home internet issues","To write all the code for every website the company owns"]
explanation: The CISO is a high-level executive responsible for aligning security programs with business goals and managing risks.

subject: CSE121
question: What is a 'Fileless Malware' attack?
options: ["An attack that doesn't use any computer files at all","Malware that lives only in the computer's RAM (memory)","A physical attack that involves stealing all user folders","Malware that converts every file on the system to a PDF"]
explanation: Fileless malware doesn't write to the disk, making it very hard for traditional antivirus to detect since no malicious file is saved.

subject: CSE121
question: How can AI-based threat detection systems improve over traditional antivirus?
options: ["By physically removing dust from the computer components","By identifying new, unknown patterns of malicious behavior","By making the computer keyboard more comfortable to use","By automatically paying all the company's monthly bills"]
explanation: Traditional AV uses signatures (lists of known viruses), while AI can spot suspicious activities even if the virus has never been seen before.

subject: CSE121
question: Which term describes 'Tailgating' in physical security?
options: ["Following an authorized person through a secure door","Attaching a tracking device to the back of a company car","Sending a chain email to every person in the building","Selling company secrets to a competitor for a profit"]
explanation: Tailgating is a simple social engineering tactic where an attacker gains entry to a restricted area by following close behind someone who has access.

subject: CSE121
question: Why should you regularly update your computer's operating system?
options: ["To get new colorful desktop wallpapers and themes","To fix security vulnerabilities (bugs) and improve safety","To make the physical screen of the computer much brighter","To ensure that all old files are automatically deleted"]
explanation: Software patches (updates) often include fixes for security holes that hackers could exploit.

subject: CSE121
question: What is 'Shadow IT' in a corporate environment?
options: ["Using IT systems that are physically painted in a dark color","Using software or hardware without the IT department's approval","A team of secret hackers hired by the company to test tools","IT support that is only available during the night hours"]
explanation: Shadow IT happens when employees use unapproved apps (like a personal Dropbox for work files), which can lead to data leaks and security risks.

subject: CSE121
question: What is the primary purpose of an 'Incident Response Plan'?
options: ["To prevent any cybersecurity incidents from ever happening","To guide an organization on how to react to and recover from a breach","To physically move all company data to a different city","To automatically fire any employee involved in a security error"]
explanation: An IR plan ensures that a company can handle a cyberattack efficiently and minimize the damage caused.

subject: CSE121
question: The Equifax data breach of 2017 was largely caused by which failure?
options: ["A physical theft of paper records from the headquarters","Failure to patch a known vulnerability in a web application","A massive flood that destroyed all the main data servers","An employee losing their physical keys in a public park"]
explanation: The breach exposed the personal info of 147 million people and happened because a standard security patch for Apache Struts was not applied.

subject: CSE121
question: Which of the following is most likely to be covered by the GDPR regulation?
options: ["The physical safety of workers in a construction zone","The protection of personal data and privacy for EU citizens","The rules for flying airplanes in international airspace","The speed limit for vehicles on city roads in the EU"]
explanation: GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) is a strict privacy law that sets rules for how personal information can be collected and used.
